content.problemStatement:
In Kotlin, there is no specific foreach loop like in some other programming languages.\
Instead, Kotlin provides a more versatile and concise way to iterate over elements of collections, including arrays, lists, sets, and more.\
You can use the *'for'* loop with the *'in'* keyword to achieve the same functionality as a *'foreach'* loop.

      syntax -   
```kotlin
                 for (element in collection) {
                    // Code to be executed for each element
                 }
```


       e.g:      
```kotlin
                 val numbers = arrayOf(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
                 // Using for loop to iterate over array elements
                 for (number in numbers) {
                    println(number)
                 }
```

In this example, we create an array numbers with integers and use the for loop to iterate over each element of the array. The loop iterates over the collection of elements (array elements, in this case) and assigns the current element to the variable number during each iteration. Then, we print the value of number.

### Task
Write a program which does the following
- Create an array $num$ which has the integers - 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80
- Output to the console the following
  - All the elements of the given array in separate lines
  - Use the "**for**" loop syntax defined above
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
